message:
fix(curate): Add float tolerance for decay thresholds + optimize conflict detection (bead 6r0k)

Problem:
- curatePlaybook() compares decayed feedback counts (floating point) to
  integer thresholds like pruneHarmfulThreshold=3
- Because feedback timestamps differ from now() by milliseconds/seconds,
  each "recent" event decays to slightly under 1.0 (e.g., 0.9999999)
- This makes sums like "3 events" become 2.9999997, causing strict >= 3
  comparisons to fail unpredictably
- Inversion/deprecation behavior was non-deterministic near thresholds,
  especially in tests and rapid feedback loops

Solution:
- Add small epsilon (0.01) to threshold comparisons where we treat
  integer thresholds as "count-like": decayedHarmful >= (threshold - ε)
- A rule marked harmful N times "right now" now behaves like N, not N-ε

Performance optimization (bonus):
- Add ConflictMeta interface to pre-compute tokens and marker flags for
  all bullets once before conflict detection loop
- New detectConflictsWithMeta() uses pre-computed data for O(n) instead
  of O(n²) marker checks
- Optimize Jaccard similarity using pre-computed token Sets with early
  skip based on size ratio (if minSize/maxSize < threshold, skip)
- Pre-compute conflict metadata once per curatePlaybook() call

Test added:
- "inverts at prune threshold even with slight decay (float tolerance)"
  uses timestamps 1 second old to reproduce the bug without the epsilon

This ensures deterministic behavior at thresholds while significantly
improving performance for large playbooks with many conflict checks.

🤖 Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code)
